channel.title: varelasaraiva.com diff log
channel.description: A log of changes to the site content

item.title: Weeknotes 202615
item.description:
+---
+title: Weeknotes 202615
+category: weeknote
+publish: true
+---
+- It was the second week of Easter holidays for H. and we decided to take the week off and spend it in the Center Parcs in the Elveden Forest, Suffolk.
+- It is very much part of the family holidays industrial-complex and it is very formatted but we liked the format and would definitely go back again. Two and five year olds are only able to enjoy about a tenth of what is on offer, so there is still lots replayability potential.
+- H. definitely had a blast: "Best holidays ever!". Good, that's what we want to hear.
+- I was hoping to do loads of stuff and move the site from beta to something more stable and complete in the late evenings whilst staying there but the competition from reading The Screwtape Letters and watching the end of the season 3 of The Chosen (mostly this), meant things are pretty much in the same state. No worries, we'll get there soon.
+- Placed an order for a tricycle for our two year old A., who is a bit of a Schumacher, and another helmet for H., one size up.
+- Now, it's time to go back to the routine of work, school and nursery. My office day count is at zero in April so it's going to be an interesting rest of the month.
\ No newline at end of file

item.title: Weeknotes 202614
item.description:
+---
+title: Weeknotes 202614
+category: weeknote
+publish: true
+---
+- The first week of H's Easter holidays. H. went to holiday club on work days, which worked reasonably well. The only downside was the pickup at 1630, meaning that work was somewhat disrupted from that time onwards. I tried to compress the days as much as possible and I think that worked well.
+- I went just once to the office this week and completed the monthly minimum for March. No contribution towards the April count means the second half of the month is going to be a crunch.
+- Next week, we'll take the week off and we're going for the first time to Center Parcs. A very welcome break because energy was running low and we need to manage it until the longer summer break, which will perhaps happen only in August.
\ No newline at end of file

item.title: Weeknotes 202613
item.description:
+---
+title: Weeknotes 202613
+category: weeknote
+publish: true
+---
+- The site is still in beta. I haven't had the time to address what is still pending because...
+- Last week was different because A. got hands, foot and mouth for the third time and had to stay at home recovering for two days, with my wife and I alternating with the nursing duties. Because she had a blocked nose, A. did not sleep much due to the troubled breathing, so we ended the week exhausted. Things are coming back to normal.
+- I finally seem to have solved a problem with the [[Pixelbook]] that has been nagging me since I've started using Linux on it. The variant with an Intel Core i5 CPU (the one I have) doesn't have a modern SSD; it has an eMMC, which is basically an SD card soldered to the motherboard that can only do one operation at a time. This means the system is very limited in terms of I/O. When running I/O intensive tasks like e.g. upgrading the system, that completely saturates the system and even low level events like refreshing what is displayed on the screen or moving the mouse cursor around has to wait. This means that sometimes, especially when these processes would run unattended in the background, the laptop would freeze for minutes on end, rendering it completely unusable. The solution is mainly using a kernel scheduler that deprioritises this sort of tasks and reserves resource for anything essential. This way, even if these I/O heavy tasks may take longer to complete, you can ensure that your system will always be responsive. I may add something about this soon, in a page somewhere.
+- We've got to the end of the first quarter of the year, surely surprising many skeptics. It's time to look again at the yearly objectives and make sure things don't start to slide.
\ No newline at end of file

item.title: Ubuntu on Pixelbook
item.description:
+---
+title: Ubuntu on Pixelbook
+created_date: 21/09/2023
+publish: true
+tags:
+  - device
+  - tutorial
+---
+*Disclaimer: This article should now be obsolete. If you think the information is still valid and links to external URLs have not rotted yet, do not follow this tutorial if you cannot afford losing your Pixelbook. I accept no liability for any loss. Posting questions in the subreddit [r/chrultrabook](https://reddit.com/r/chrultrabook) is your best chance of getting expert help.*
+
+## Introduction
+
+My [[pixelbook|Google Pixelbook]] expires on June 2024. I have decided I will not replace the hardware, so to replace [[ChromeOS]], I have to reflash the BIOS, format partitions and install Linux. I could do this next year but I actually missed using Linux and [[GNOME]] as my daily driver after years with [[ChromeOS]] and i3.
+
+I didn't expect an easy setup but I expected a stable experience once it was set up. This article describes how I've managed to install and use [[Ubuntu]] on a [[pixelbook|Google Pixelbook]] as it's sole operating system. Beware, it's not for the faint of heart.
+
+## Preparation
+
+Besides thorough research, I personally didn't spend too much time preparing, possibly because [[ChromeOS]] is very oriented towards using the Google services and the Google Drive, so most of the data was already located in the cloud. 
+
+In any case, I would suggest backing up all your local data to the Google Drive. It should be a matter of dragging and dropping folders in the file manager. Make sure you let the folders sync completely with the cloud otherwise, once the user data is erased, the data will be lost.
+
+Secondly, we need to create a bootable USB stick with a Linux distribution ISO installer. I have chosen [[Ubuntu]] Desktop 22.04 LTS for this case. I have used another Linux machine to download the ISO and burn it to the USB stick. Once the ISO was downloaded, I ran the command below to create the bootable USB stick:
+
+
+```bash
+dd if=Downloads/ubuntu-22.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso of=/dev/sdb bs=4M status=progress conv=fdatasync
+```
+
+The device to use in the `of` field - `/dev/sdb`  in my case - should be obvious by running `lsblk` in the terminal. I used an adapter between USB-A and USB-C to plug the USB stick to the [[pixelbook]]. There are many other ways of creating a bootable USB stick. In Windows, Rufus seems to be a popular choice. I've read reports selecting the 'dd image' mode is important later on.
+
+Now, on to the hard stuff.
+
+## Developer mode
+
+Despite of [[ChromeOS]] being Linux under the bonnet, the [[pixelbook|Google Pixelbook]] is very well locked up and secure. It has proprietary BIOS, firmwares, etc. Therefore, it's not possible by default to sideload an installer of an alternative operating system and install it side by side or in place of ChromeOS. One has to get access and reflash the ROM with an open BIOS like Coreboot to be able to do so. 
+
+I have used the Firmware Utility Script by [MrChromebox](https://twitter.com/mrchromebox) described in [his site](https://mrchromebox.tech) to install the firmware. This script must be run in the `crosh` terminal. The terminal in the `crostini` container does not have full access to the hardware, so it's impossible to flash the ROM from there. To get access to the `crosh` shell, the [[pixelbook]] must be in developer mode. One of the side effects of enabling the developer mode is essentially doing a factory reset to the [[pixelbook]], so at this point you should be prepared to erase all local data. 
+
+The developer mode is accessed via the recovery menu. To reboot to recovery, press and hold `Esc + Refresh` and then press the power button. Once you see the recovery menu, you can let go of `Esc + Refresh`, as described [here](https://support.google.com/pixelbook/answer/9134768?hl=en#zippy=%2Cstep-enter-recovery-mode) in step 3. To enable developer mode, press `Ctrl + D` and confirm, as described [here](https://mrchromebox.tech/#devmode).
+
+The next time the [[pixelbook]] is started, it will show a welcome screen like it's being switched on for the first time. I believe the `crosh` terminal can be invoked from the first screen with a `Ctrl + Alt + T`. Otherwise, you'll have to configure an account, login normally and finally invoke `crosh` using the same combination from there.
+
+## Removing write protection and flashing BIOS
+
+Unfortunately, we can't just run the script and flash the ROM, as the ROM is write-protected at hardware-level. To disable the write protection, we have to simply disconnect the battery, a process that involves removing the back panel - where the battery is glued on - and disconnecting a flat cable from the motherboard, a process very well described in [this iFixit article](https://www.ifixit.com/Guide/Google+Pixelbook+Battery+(Back+Panel)+Replacement/103036). 
+
+It's understandable if you don't feel confident proceeding with this madness. At this point, you can still boot to recovery, disable developer mode and restart normally to [[ChromeOS]]. There is [another method](https://wiki.mrchromebox.tech/Firmware_Write_Protect#Disabling_WP_on_CR50_Devices_via_CCD) described in MrChromebox's website that should be less invasive but requires a special debug USB cable, which I have not tried.
+
+Assuming my readership was brave enough to go ahead with opening the Pixelbook and disconnecting the battery, it is possible now to start the Pixelbook, launch the `crosh` with `Ctrl + Alt + T`, type `shell`, launch the Firmware Utility Script with 
+
+```bash
+cd; curl -LO mrchromebox.tech/firmware-util.sh && sudo bash firmware-util.sh
+``` 
+
+as described in [here](https://mrchromebox.tech/#fwscript), and select `Install/Update UEFI (Full ROM) Firmware`. This is the last time you'll see [[ChromeOS]] in this [[pixelbook]], before shutting down.
+
+The battery can now be reconnected to the motherboard and the back panel reattached, to enable write-protection again.
+
+## Installing Ubuntu and mitigating some quirks
+
+In the next reboot, you'll be greeted with the Coreboot logo. Chances are that you've rebooted with the USB stick plugged in but it is not being detected in the Coreboot boot menu. The solution for me was to go back to the main/root menu, unplug the USB stick, wait a couple of seconds, plug it back again, wait ~3 seconds and check the boot menu again. It didn't work straight way with me and this could be quite distressing until it works.
+
+Once you'll see an USB-related entry in the boot menu and select it, things will then become quite familiar if you've installed [[Ubuntu]] before. In my case, during the whole installation process, the picture was flipped upside down. This shouldn't prevent completing the installation, if we ignore the awkwardness. In the first boot after the installation the picture was oriented correctly.
+
+By now, you should have everything working except, I think, sound and the top row of the keyboard. I have not tested whether input events from the stylus are being recognised but intend to do so soon. It would be great if the stylus still worked with it for some ad-hoc whiteboarding.
+
+To fix the sound and the keyboard, I have followed [this thread](https://reddit.com/r/chrultrabook/s/j4oRatD9Aw) in Reddit. The important part entails installing the packages `pixelbook-alsa` and `pixelbook-keybd` from [this folder](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1POOaM7r1TnH_p7ONHPNFnFeIrFtueFw_) in Google Drive. I have not tried the `pixelbook-sensors` package. Sound and keyboard should now work out of the box.
+
+The only issue I have at the moment is, whenever I plug headphones in or out, the sound device is not switched automatically. It has to be done manually in the Settings > Sound dialogue, which is a bit a annoying. I'll try to fix this issue once [[Ubuntu]] 23.10 comes out.
+
+Other than that, it has been running quite stable so far, [[GNOME]] is a very polished experience and, in the age of PWAs and the cloud, I don't feel limited in any way. It would be great to have more than 8 GB of RAM for more browser tabs.
\ No newline at end of file

item.title: Weeknotes 202511
item.description:
+---
+title: Weeknotes 202511
+created_date: 16/03/2025
+publish: true
+category: weeknote
+---
+
+- It was today the first Formula 1 Grand Prix of the season in Melbourne. I’m watching it as I write this week’s notes. It is being quite entertaining. The McLaren seems to be the class of the field, confirming the suspicions of the pre-season testing. It seems this year’s car is very good at not putting too much strain in the tires, slowing the rate of decay in performance and allowing the drivers to push for longer.
+- I was confident at the end of last year that very soon I’d have more time and energy because nights with the 1 year old were going to improve but it’s already mid-March and it seems the year is flying by and I haven’t crossed anything from the 2025 objectives yet.
+- I have been listening to the remix of Severance soundtrack, [Music To Refine To](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JRnDYB28bL8), while working and it’s pretty good. I’ve never watched the show but everything I’ve seen about it looks great, right up my alley. On the other hand, I get the sense it would be a depressing watch as the premise hits too close to home.
+- There’ll be more next week - see you then!
\ No newline at end of file

item.title: Weeknotes 202612
item.description:
-publish: false
+publish: true
-Lorem Ipsum
\ No newline at end of file
+> There’ll be more next week - see you then!
+
+... And in the end, there was not more [[weeknotes-202511|next week]] or anything else for over a year. To cut the story short, because this is not a 2025 review, life happened. 2025 was not a bad year, by any means, but it was a hard working year. Normal parenting stuff - nothing out of the ordinary. Years usually start optimistically and I was convinced A's sleeping patterns would improve like H's around the second year and we, the parents, would be less sleep deprived, but this time it was different.
+
+I have been planning (in my mind) a rewrite of the static site generator for most of 2025 and I finally decided to roll up my sleeves during the Christmas break. The main new features are
+
+1. Incremental builds
+2. Using git to save checkpoints and derive created and modified dates, instead of relying on the OS filesystem's `ctime` and `mtime`
+3. Generate RSS feeds 
+
+I had no idea what I was getting into with the incremental builds and how infinitely more complex the codebase has become. Fast forward to last week, it felt most functionality was there and the most obvious bugs were fixed. It is still in beta but I'm doing a soft launch, not announcing for now in any of the usual channels. I think it's just a matter of updating and  polishing some of the slash pages and it should ready for the general audience.
+
+I am hoping this will be a solid foundation of a slowly evolving platform. Developing the static site generator has been fun but now it's time to turn my attention to actually using it - writing content. 
\ No newline at end of file

item.title: Weeknotes 202511
item.description:
+---
+title: Weeknotes 202511
+created_date: 16/03/2025
+publish: true
+category: weeknote
+---
+
+- It was today the first Formula 1 Grand Prix of the season in Melbourne. I’m watching it as I write this week’s notes. It is being quite entertaining. The McLaren seems to be the class of the field, confirming the suspicions of the pre-season testing. It seems this year’s car is very good at not putting too much strain in the tires, slowing the rate of decay in performance and allowing the drivers to push for longer.
+- I was confident at the end of last year that very soon I’d have more time and energy because nights with the 1 year old were going to improve but it’s already mid-March and it seems the year is flying by and I haven’t crossed anything from the 2025 objectives yet.
+- I have been listening to the remix of Severance soundtrack, [Music To Refine To](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JRnDYB28bL8), while working and it’s pretty good. I’ve never watched the show but everything I’ve seen about it looks great, right up my alley. On the other hand, I get the sense it would be a depressing watch as the premise hits too close to home.
+- There’ll be more next week - see you then!
\ No newline at end of file

item.title: Weeknotes 202508
item.description:
+---
+title: Weeknotes 202508
+created_date: 23/02/2025
+publish: true
+category: weeknote
+---
+- Cold symptoms carried over from last week and turned into something quite annoying.
+- Ended up not doing my weekly pilgrimage to the Paddington office, which I enjoy, to avoid spreading viral load and hopefully to recover quicker.
+- Added some polish to the [[colophon|wiki]]. The front page is improved and the layout in mobile should feel a bit more comfortable.
+- It’s half-term week here in the UK and that usually means juggling a bit more family stuff but this time we’ve booked holiday clubs with decent open and closing times so it has worked well.
+- We are going to spend a week in Lisbon around Easter but we need to nail those dates down so that we can start organising the trip.
+- [Obsidian is now free for commercial use](https://obsidian.md/blog/free-for-work/), so I can move away from OneNote at work, which I’ve been using since I’ve been given a slap on the wrist for using [[Obsidian]] without a licence a while ago.
+- I find it hard to believe things got to this point but [Apple disabled E2E encryption for some of their services for UK users](https://www.theverge.com/news/617273/apple-removes-encryption-advanced-data-protection-adp-uk-spying-backdoor).
+- Looking forward for the next week, cold symptoms-free!
\ No newline at end of file

item.title: Weeknotes 202510
item.description:
+---
+title: Weeknotes 202510
+created_date: 09/03/2025
+publish: true
+category: weeknote
+---
+
+- It’s March, the meteorological spring has started, the weather has been lovely in this part of the planet. After the snow bells, crocuses and daffodils are everywhere and cherry trees are blossoming.
+- There was Formula 1 pre-season testing in Bahrain and this week we have the first race of the season in Melbourne. I have a lot to say about this but will spare my readership and just write a few commonplace ideas: the last season before major changes in regulation is usually the best with performance convergence across the field, where usually everyone asks themselves *Why are we changing the formula if this is so good?*. On the other hand, McLaren seems to have made another step forward in performance — still to be confirmed — and this season could be a duel between Norris and Piastri, reminiscent of Hamilton vs Rosberg in 2014. 
+- I’ve ordered a [Keychron K1 Pro](https://www.keychron.uk/products/keychron-k1-pro-qmk-via-wireless-custom-mechanical-keyboard-iso-layout-collection?variant=45743748022442) with PBT keycaps and brown switches. Since I spend so much time typing, thought it would be nice to use something substantially better than the £20 mechanical keyboard I’ve been using in the past few years. It’s all about the small pleasures.
+- We used to slog almost 7 months straight after the Christmas break and spend the first holiday period around the end of June but for the first time in 18 years we’ll be spending Easter in Lisbon, to better align with the school holidays. I think it’s great.
+- Wish you all a fantastic week!
\ No newline at end of file

item.title: Pixelbook
item.description:
+---
+title: Pixelbook
+created_date: 21/09/2023
+publish: true
+tags:
+  - device
+---
+The Pixelbook was an ultrabook launched on 2017 by Google, running [[ChromeOS]]. It had 6 years of software support and mine expired in June 2024. I have decided to [[ubuntu-on-pixelbook|wipe it and install Ubuntu on it]] around September 2023.
+
+![[Pasted image 20230921232931.png]]
+Photo by Google.
+
+I've been a happy user of a Google Pixelbook for a few years. It has brilliant hardware and software. It's not a regular laptop, as it uses [[ChromeOS]] as an operating system. I would describe it as a computer for the mobile phone age: instantly on, simple, user friendly, very low maintenance and it Just Works. This might not be a big deal in 2023 but, in 2017, I can assure you it was.
+
+Unfortunately, as for other main vendors, it also has finite software support from the vendor. Every [[ChromeOS]] device has an expiration date, when it's no longer possible to install any updates. This wouldn't be necessarily a problem for 'regular' operating systems, where we can at least keep the apps up to date. In ChromeOS, the Chrome browser is a central part of the operating system and can't be installed externally. This means, after the laptop expires, it will continue to function but it will not get the latest features and, more importantly, it will no longer receive the latest security patches, thus potentially exposing private data or be exposed to other attacks. 
+
+It is possible to install Android Chrome from the Google Play store or install desktop Linux Chrome from the Crostini container but none of these would provide a first-class experience and important integration with the rest of the operating system would be lost. Interestingly, it is possible to have a lifetime of well supported Chrome in a device using any other operating system but not on a Google device, with a Google operating system.
+
+https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=220705
\ No newline at end of file
